questions.stem: A 30-year-old male from sub-Saharan Africa presents with a 4-week history of cough, haemoptysis, night sweats, and weight loss. Chest X-ray shows a cavity in the right upper zone. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The combination of apical cavitation, haemoptysis, night sweats, and weight loss in a patient from a TB-endemic area is classic for pulmonary tuberculosis. Lung abscess typically has fever and purulent sputum but less systemic upset; bronchial carcinoma would not cause night sweats; sarcoidosis rarely cavitates; aspergilloma usually forms in pre-existing cavities.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old student presents with a 2-week history of dry cough, low-grade fever, and erythema nodosum on her shins. Chest X-ray shows bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy with erythema nodosum is pathognomonic for sarcoidosis (Löfgren syndrome). Primary TB typically causes unilateral hilar lymphadenopathy, and erythema nodosum is rare. Lymphoma may cause mediastinal but not symmetric hilar adenopathy; histoplasmosis is uncommon in the UK; tuberculous lymphadenitis usually presents with cervical nodes.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with known pulmonary tuberculosis on rifampicin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide, and ethambutol for 3 weeks presents with sudden worsening dyspnoea, pleuritic chest pain, and a dry cough. Chest X-ray shows a right-sided pneumothorax. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Spontaneous pneumothorax due to TB cavity rupture
questions.explanation: In a patient with cavitary PTB, a sudden pneumothorax suggests rupture of a cavity into the pleural space. Empyema would have fever and effusion; bronchopleural fistula usually presents with persistent air leak; pulmonary embolism unlikely without risk factors; pericarditis would have chest pain but no pneumothorax.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old HIV-positive man (CD4 80 cells/µL) presents with a 6-week history of cough, weight loss, and fever. Chest X-ray is normal. Sputum smear is positive for acid-fast bacilli. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: In HIV with advanced immunosuppression, TB can present with a normal chest X-ray due to poor granuloma formation. Positive sputum smear confirms TB. MAC is less common and usually has disseminated disease; PJP would show bilateral interstitial infiltrates and is smear-negative; histoplasmosis is rare in the UK; bronchial carcinoma unlikely in this age with positive smear.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old woman presents with a 2-month history of dry cough, weight loss, and back pain. Chest X-ray reveals a right apical opacity and destruction of the T6 vertebral body. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The combination of an apical opacity and vertebral destruction suggests Pott's disease (tuberculous spondylitis) from contiguous spread of pulmonary TB. Lung cancer metastasis is less likely to cause isolated vertebral destruction with apical opacity; pneumonia with osteomyelitis is atypical; sarcoidosis rarely invades bone; aspergillosis usually occurs in pre-existing cavities.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man from Somalia presents with a 3-week history of cough, night sweats, and weight loss. He has no past medical history and is not on any medications. Clinical examination is unremarkable. What is the single most appropriate initial investigation?
questions.explanation: A chest X-ray is the recommended first-line investigation for suspected pulmonary tuberculosis. It may show characteristic upper lobe changes. Sputum smear is confirmatory but not the first test; IGRA indicates infection but not active disease; CT and bronchoscopy are reserved for uncertain cases or when sputum is unavailable.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man presents with a productive cough and low-grade fever. His CXR shows a cavitating lesion in the right upper lobe. An IGRA is positive. What is the single most appropriate next test to confirm active tuberculosis?
questions.explanation: NICE recommends a molecular test (e.g., Xpert MTB/RIF) on the first sputum sample for rapid diagnosis and rifampicin resistance detection. While sputum culture is the gold standard, it takes weeks. CT is not diagnostic; bronchoscopy is reserved if sputum unavailable; Mantoux and IGRA are already positive.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old woman being treated for pulmonary tuberculosis develops sudden-onset dyspnoea, with dullness and reduced breath sounds at the right base. A chest X-ray confirms a large right pleural effusion. What is the single most appropriate diagnostic test for the effusion?
questions.explanation: In suspected tuberculous pleural effusion, elevated pleural fluid ADA >40 U/L is highly sensitive and specific, and is recommended by NICE as first-line. Pleural biopsy is more invasive and reserved for when ADA is equivocal. CT-PA rules out pulmonary embolism but not TB; bronchoscopy is not needed.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with known pulmonary tuberculosis completes 3 months of standard therapy but remains symptomatic with cough and weight loss. His initial sputum culture was positive and drug susceptibility testing was not performed. What is the single most appropriate test to differentiate treatment failure from drug-resistant disease?
options: Drug susceptibility testing on the original isolate
questions.explanation: Drug susceptibility testing (DST) on the initial culture isolate or on a repeat sample is essential to identify resistance. Repeat culture alone confirms ongoing positivity but not the cause. CT and CXR show structural changes but not resistance. Bronchoscopy is not needed.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old pregnant woman (12 weeks gestation) from Pakistan presents with cough and night sweats. Chest X-ray with shielding shows an apical infiltrate. She cannot spontaneously produce sputum and does not wish to undergo sputum induction. What is the single most appropriate diagnostic test to confirm active tuberculosis?
questions.explanation: In patients who cannot expectorate and decline sputum induction, bronchoscopy is the recommended next step to obtain lower respiratory samples. IGRA indicates infection only; CT is not diagnostic; Xpert requires sputum; gastric lavage is primarily used in children. Bronchoscopy, while slightly more invasive, is preferred in this scenario.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old man from Nigeria presents with a 3-week history of cough, night sweats, and weight loss. Sputum smear is positive for acid-fast bacilli. He has no known drug allergies or comorbidities. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment regimen?
options: Rifampicin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide, ethambutol for 2 months, then rifampicin and isoniazid for 4 months
Rifampicin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide, ethambutol for 6 months
questions.explanation: The standard 6-month regimen for drug-sensitive TB in the UK is 4 drugs (rifampicin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide, ethambutol) for 2 months, followed by 2 drugs (rifampicin, isoniazid) for 4 months. Option 1 is correct; the other options are incorrect durations or regimens (e.g., monotherapy, shorter regimens, or BPaL reserved for MDR-TB).

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man with known Child-Pugh B cirrhosis is diagnosed with sputum smear-positive pulmonary TB. His bilirubin is 45 μmol/L and ALT is 80 U/L. Which first-line anti-TB drug is relatively contraindicated in this patient?
questions.explanation: Pyrazinamide is the most hepatotoxic first-line agent and is relatively contraindicated in significant hepatic impairment. Rifampicin and isoniazid also cause hepatotoxicity but are used with caution; ethambutol and streptomycin are not hepatotoxic.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman on standard TB therapy for 4 weeks presents with jaundice and bilirubin 120 μmol/L. Other causes of hepatitis are excluded. Which drug is most likely responsible and should be permanently discontinued?
questions.explanation: Isoniazid is the most common cause of drug-induced hepatitis in TB treatment, often requiring permanent discontinuation. Rifampicin and pyrazinamide can also cause hepatitis but are less frequently the sole culprit; ethambutol and streptomycin are not hepatotoxic.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman from Moldova was treated for smear-positive TB with standard quadruple therapy. After 2 months, sputum cultures remain positive and drug susceptibility testing shows resistance to rifampicin and isoniazid. What is the most appropriate regimen to start now?
questions.explanation: For MDR-TB (resistant to rifampicin and isoniazid), UK guidelines recommend an all-oral regimen with bedaquiline, linezolid, and a fluoroquinolone such as moxifloxacin. Options 1 and 2 are ineffective; option 4 includes injectable (not first-line oral); option 5 uses pyrazinamide and levofloxacin but lacks bedaquiline and linezolid.

questions.stem: A 26-year-old pregnant woman at 20 weeks gestation is diagnosed with pulmonary TB. She has normal liver and renal function. Which anti-TB drug is contraindicated?
questions.explanation: Streptomycin is an aminoglycoside that crosses the placenta and can cause ototoxic damage to the fetal eighth cranial nerve, leading to hearing loss. It is contraindicated in pregnancy. The other drugs are safe or used with caution.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man presents with a 4-week history of cough, weight loss, and night sweats. He is febrile. Chest X-ray shows a right apical cavity with surrounding infiltrates. Sputum smear for acid-fast bacilli is negative, but nucleic acid amplification test (NAAT) is positive for Mycobacterium tuberculosis complex. What is the single most appropriate next step?
options: Perform a CT chest to further characterise the cavity
Refer to respiratory physician for bronchoscopy
Wait for sputum culture and drug susceptibility results before starting treatment
Start standard quadruple therapy (rifampicin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide, ethambutol)
questions.explanation: NAAT positivity confirms TB with high specificity, and the clinical/radiological picture is highly suggestive. UK guidelines (NICE NG33) recommend starting empirical treatment without waiting for culture, especially given the cavity and systemic symptoms.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman with pulmonary TB on standard quadruple therapy (rifampicin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide, ethambutol) for 2 weeks develops jaundice and dark urine. Liver function tests show bilirubin 60 µmol/L, ALT 450 U/L, AST 380 U/L. She is haemodynamically stable. What is the single most appropriate next step?
options: Reduce the dose of isoniazid and continue other drugs
Switch to a non-hepatotoxic regimen (e.g., fluoroquinolone-based)
Add ursodeoxycholic acid as a hepatoprotective agent
Check serology for hepatitis A, B, C, and E
questions.explanation: Drug-induced liver injury is common with first-line TB drugs. With jaundice and transaminases >5 times upper limit, immediate cessation of all hepatotoxic drugs is essential to prevent acute liver failure.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with known cavitary pulmonary TB suddenly coughs up approximately 300 mL of bright red blood. He is now tachycardic (HR 115) and hypotensive (BP 85/50). What is the single most appropriate next step?
options: Resuscitate with intravenous fluids and call for urgent interventional radiology/thoracic surgical review
Perform an urgent bronchoscopy to identify the bleeding source
Request an urgent CT pulmonary angiogram to rule out pulmonary embolism
Start intravenous antibiotics to cover aspiration pneumonia
questions.explanation: Massive haemoptysis with haemodynamic instability requires immediate resuscitation and urgent specialist intervention (bronchial artery embolisation or surgery). Bronchoscopy is not first-line in this setting due to risk of worsening bleeding and limited view.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman with known miliary TB develops headache, vomiting, and a GCS drop from 15 to 13. She has neck stiffness but no focal neurology. What is the single most appropriate next step?
options: Start intravenous acyclovir for possible viral encephalitis
Request an urgent CT head before lumbar puncture
Start antituberculous therapy and intravenous dexamethasone
Give intravenous mannitol for raised intracranial pressure
questions.explanation: In suspected TB meningitis with reduced GCS and signs of raised ICP, CT head is needed first to exclude space-occupying lesions or hydrocephalus before safe lumbar puncture.

questions.stem: A 24-year-old pregnant woman at 14 weeks gestation has a positive interferon-gamma release assay (IGRA) as part of a contact tracing screen. She is asymptomatic and her chest X-ray (with abdominal shield) is normal. She was born in India and moved to the UK 2 years ago. What is the single most appropriate next step?
options: Defer all investigations and treatment until after delivery
Refer to a TB specialist clinic for assessment and shared decision-making
Start isoniazid monotherapy for latent TB infection
Start standard quadruple therapy for active TB
Perform a CT chest to rule out subclinical disease
questions.explanation: Latent TB in pregnancy requires specialist input to weigh risks and benefits of treatment. NICE recommends referral to a TB specialist; treatment may be deferred until postpartum or initiated with close monitoring after discussion.

questions.stem: A 24-year-old medical student presents with 3 weeks of cough, fever, and night sweats. CXR shows right upper lobe cavitation. Which single finding most strongly indicates that he is infectious and requires urgent respiratory isolation?
options: Positive sputum smear for acid-fast bacilli
Positive sputum culture for M. tuberculosis
History of contact with a TB patient
questions.explanation: A positive sputum smear for acid-fast bacilli indicates a high bacillary load and high infectivity, necessitating immediate respiratory isolation. Sputum culture takes weeks. Cavitation is suggestive but not definitive for active infectious TB.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with known pulmonary TB on treatment for 2 months presents with sudden massive hemoptysis (500 ml in 24 hours). What is the most likely life-threatening complication?
questions.explanation: Massive hemoptysis in TB is most commonly due to rupture of a Rasmussen aneurysm (a pseudoaneurysm of a pulmonary artery branch) or bronchial artery erosion. Bronchopleural fistula may cause air-leak but not massive bleeding. Empyema and bronchiectasis are less acute.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with active pulmonary TB develops increasing dyspnoea and pleuritic chest pain. CXR shows a large left pleural effusion. Which finding on pleural fluid analysis is a red flag for tuberculous empyema requiring urgent drainage?
questions.explanation: A pleural fluid pH <7.2 is a key indicator of empyema (complicated parapneumonic effusion) and mandates urgent drainage. High ADA and lymphocytosis are typical of tuberculous pleurisy but not immediate red flags for drainage.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old healthcare worker with no prior TB treatment presents with cough, fever, and a CXR showing miliary nodules. What is the most important immediate concern?
options: Possibility of disseminated TB with CNS involvement
Risk of spreading infection to colleagues
questions.explanation: Miliary TB represents haematogenous dissemination; CNS involvement (TB meningitis) is a life-threatening complication that must be excluded urgently. While infectivity and drug resistance are important, CNS involvement is the most immediate danger.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old pregnant woman at 16 weeks gestation is diagnosed with smear-positive pulmonary TB. Which finding would most urgently prompt consideration of termination of pregnancy?
options: Positive culture after 2 months of treatment
questions.explanation: Miliary (disseminated) TB in pregnancy carries high maternal and fetal mortality, so termination may be considered to allow aggressive treatment. Drug resistance and hepatitis are serious but not immediate indications for termination.
